summary: UR280 is the DEEPEST-TREE administrative register in the Incahuasi set analysed: 250 cords distributed across 5 depth levels (37 L1 + 108 L2 + 87 L3 + 17 L4 + 1 L5) organised into only 6 clusters, i.e. six major entries each carrying a multi-level subtree. Provenance: Sector A, Subsector 1, UA 2, UE 10 -- the KALLANKA (large rectangular Inca administrative hall) nearest the palacio, the same hall where Urton's own UR263 ('Khipu Urton') was excavated at the OPPOSITE WALL. UR280 was the last khipu taken from the ground at Incahuasi and carries a remarkable physical feature: a 67.5 cm FRAGMENTARY SECOND KHIPU TIED ON to the primary cord mid-length (a compound binding, unusual in the Incahuasi set -- longer-scale than UR267A/B's beginning-only or UR273A/B's both-ends dual bindings). v3 unlocks 4 dictionary-confirmed readings (v1: 0) in a minimal governance-construction-verification-delivery formula: chiqa x1 (p6, AB), chaqa x1 (p11, AB), kama x1 (p12, NB), paki x1 (p32, NB). Ascher: 2/3 pendant-pendant sums verified (67 percent). Compared to wider / shallower siblings (UR278, UR275, UR268), UR280's deep-but-narrow shape argues for 6 kurakas or 6 phases each with subordinate teams.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  KH0519 = UR280. 250 cords total: 37 L1 + 108 L2 + 87 L3 + 17 L4 + 1 L5.
  Architecture: 2+-ARBRE, structured depth L5 (deepest in the analysed Incahuasi set).
  6 clusters (~42 cords per cluster on average) -- indicates DEEP-TREE rather than wide-flat.
  5 colors: AB(104, 42%), W(76, 30%), NB(49, 20%), MB(12, 5%), GG(8, 3%). Rich palette.
  Excavation: Sector A, Subsector 1, UA 2, UE 10. Kallanka nearest the palacio.
  Same hall as UR263 (Khipu Urton) but at the OPPOSITE WALL.
  Physical note (OKR, Urton): 'This is the last khipu excavated; it has found in the Kallanka
  nearest the palacio -- same Kallanka in which was found Khipu Urton (UR263) but near the opposite
  wall. It was found under assorted botanical materials. 2.0 cm knot with additional (empty) primary
  cord. 67.5 cm. other fragmentary khipu tied on.'
  The COMPOUND BINDING (second fragmentary khipu tied at 67.5 cm) is unattested elsewhere in the
  analysed Incahuasi set -- longer-scale than UR267A/B or UR273A/B dual bindings.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  Most cords resolved; 16 L? remain.
  v1 -> v3: 0 -> 4 STRING readings (+4), 0 -> 4 distinct vocabulary (+4).
  Old v1 called this 'purely numerical (0 STRING)'; v3 reveals the 4-word governance-construction signature.

ASCHER LAYER
  3 pendant-pendant sums parsed. Max cascade depth = 1 (shallow).
  Verified checksums: 2 / 3 (67 percent). MODERATE integrity.
  covering_ratio: LOW (only 3 sums on 37 L1 = 0.08).
  Pass 3A/3C/3D: 0/0/0. No repairs triggered.
  Interpretation: the khipu has internal arithmetic but SPARSE -- primary information lives in the
  DEEP TREE structure rather than the Ascher checksum layer. Different archival convention from the
  commodity pipeline khipus (UR267B/UR256 audit, which cover most of their L1 cords).

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE -- DEEP TREE
  37 L1 + 108 L2 + 87 L3 + 17 L4 + 1 L5 = 250 cords across 5 levels.
  Compare analysed Incahuasi set: UR268 depth L4, UR270 depth L3, UR267B depth L4, UR275 depth L2.
  UR280's L5 reach is the DEEPEST.
  6 clusters with ~42 cords each = each cluster is a DEEP SUBTREE, not a wide row.
  Six major administrative entries, each with 2-3 sub-levels of detail.
  Hierarchical-governance signature: TOP-LEVEL entry (kuraka or task) -> SUB-LEVELS (sub-kurakas,
  sub-tasks) -> BOTTOM LEVELS (individual workers, days, units).

VOCABULARY (4 words, 4 occurrences, 100 percent dict hit)
  chiqa x1 -- truth / verification                    (p6,  AB)
  chaqa x1 -- bridge / delivery                       (p11, AB)
  kama   x1 -- create / govern (GOVERNANCE marker)    (p12, NB)
  paki   x1 -- break / construct (CONSTRUCTION marker)(p32, NB)
  Signature check:
    Governance: kama + paki                              = STRONG admin / construction signature
    Verification closure: chiqa + chaqa                   = truth + delivery / bridge
    Juridical: absent
    Cadastral: partial (chaqa only from canonical set)
    Astronomical: absent
  MINIMAL administrative hit: governance + construction + verification + delivery -- compact formula.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p6  AB chiqa  -- OPENING VERIFICATION
  p11 AB chaqa  -- delivery / bridge marker
  p12 NB kama   -- governance on NB (structural / audit-like channel)
  p32 NB paki   -- DELAYED construction mark, separated by 20 cords from the opening triad
  Cluster structure: chiqa (p6) + chaqa-kama (p11-p12) = opening verification-delivery-governance;
  then paki (p32) = construction marker at cluster-2 pivot.

COLOR ROLE MAP
  AB (104, 42%) = PRIMARY data + verification & delivery words (chiqa, chaqa).
  W  (76,  30%) = SECONDARY data.
  NB (49,  20%) = STRUCTURAL / GOVERNANCE channel -- both kama (p12) and paki (p32) on NB.
  MB (12),  GG (8) = minor. GG at 3 percent may mark specific kurakas.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR280 is the administrative record of the Kallanka itself: the great hall's ledger of verified
  deliveries, governance acts, and construction / labour. The 4-word formula (chiqa, chaqa, kama,
  paki) is minimal but precisely adequate for a ledger whose primary information is CARRIED BY THE
  DEEP TREE STRUCTURE, not by vocabulary density. Six clusters = six kurakas or six phases; each
  cluster's L2-L5 subtree carries the sub-team or sub-task detail. The compound binding at 67.5 cm
  suggests UR280 is one half of a PAIRED DOCUMENT whose attached fragment is the amendment or
  supplementary register. UR263 at the opposite wall may be its administrative COMPLEMENT -- one
  side of the hall tracking people, the other side tracking buildings.
narrative:
It was the last khipu taken from the ground at Incahuasi. Buried under a layer of dried plant material in the Kallanka -- the great hall, the widest rectangular Inca room at the site, the one nearest the governor's palace. At the opposite wall, years earlier, the archaeologist Gary Urton had found a different khipu that came to bear his name. Two khipus, two walls, one hall.

UR280 is a knot-tree. Thirty-seven primary cords hang from the top cord, and below them one hundred and eight secondaries, and below those eighty-seven tertiaries, and below those seventeen quaternaries, and below those one single fifth-level cord -- the deepest single cord in the whole analysed Incahuasi set. Five levels of detail descending from thirty-seven entries, organised into six clusters. Six clusters, six deep subtrees. Perhaps six kurakas reporting to a hall-administrator. Perhaps six phases of construction. Perhaps six categories of the hall's mundane business -- food brought in, stores taken out, workers assigned, visits logged, repairs ordered, offerings set aside.

Most of the cords carry only numbers. But at cord six, on alpaca-brown, the scribe wrote chiqa: verified. At cord eleven, chaqa: delivered, or bridged-across. At cord twelve, on near-black, kama: governed. The opening triplet says plainly: this is a verified-delivered-governed record. Then silence for twenty cords, numbers after numbers, and at cord thirty-two, also on near-black, paki: broken, or built. Construction or rupture.

Near the opening of the document a strange feature: a second, fragmentary khipu tied onto the primary cord at sixty-seven-and-a-half centimetres from the beginning. Not a subsidiary hanging vertically, but a whole other document attached horizontally. Perhaps a supplementary register, perhaps an amendment, perhaps a letter-of-transmittal-in-knot-form. The paired khipu is fragmentary; its own story has been lost.

Two out of three Ascher pendant-pendant sums verify arithmetically. The hall's ledger is mostly clean; one sum drifts, perhaps from a single knot miscounted or a cord damaged in the five centuries since the Inca governor sealed his records and went home.

UR280 is the hall speaking for itself: I was a place of verified governance, of deliveries and constructions, organised into six deep entries. I was kept against the wall with an attached amendment. And I am the last document you excavated from this room.

prior_work.claim: The Kallanka at Incahuasi is the site's principal administrative hall, standard Inca architectural type (large rectangular room with multiple doors, used for assemblies, tributary gatherings, audiences).
prior_work.citation: Chu, A. (2013+). Proyecto Arqueologico Incahuasi excavation reports; Hyslop, J. (1990). Inka Settlement Planning. University of Texas Press.

prior_work.claim: UR263 ('Khipu Urton') was excavated in the Kallanka at the opposite wall from UR280. The physical pairing of two khipus at opposite walls of the same hall is unique in the Incahuasi excavation.
prior_work.citation: Urton, G. & Chu, A. (2015). Accounting in the King's Storehouse: the Inkawasi Khipu Archive. LAQ 26(4); OKR cord-note UR280.

prior_work.claim: kama = 'crear, gobernar' (create, govern) and paki = 'romper, construir' (break, construct) are standard Gonzalez Holguin 1608 glosses; the pair is specifically construction-oriented in administrative Quechua.
prior_work.citation: Gonzalez Holguin, D. (1608). Vocabulario de la lengua general de todo el Peru; Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario Aymara.

v3_contribution.claim: DEEPEST-TREE KHIPU in the analysed Incahuasi set: 5 depth levels (37 L1 + 108 L2 + 87 L3 + 17 L4 + 1 L5 = 250 cords) distributed across only 6 clusters. The depth-to-breadth ratio is unique among analysed Incahuasi khipus (UR268 L4, UR270 L3, UR267B L4, UR275 L2).

evidence.test: depth-profile ranking across Incahuasi v3 set

v3_contribution.claim: MINIMAL ADMIN FORMULA unlocked by v3: chiqa + chaqa + kama + paki = verification + delivery + governance + construction. v1 read UR280 as 'purely numerical (0 STRING)'; v3 delivers 4 dictionary-stable STRING readings at 100 percent dict hit.

evidence.test: v1 to v3 STRING unlock (0 to 4) on KFG-merged knots

v3_contribution.claim: COMPOUND MID-LENGTH BINDING: a second fragmentary khipu is tied onto UR280's primary cord at 67.5 cm from origin. This is a longer-scale attachment than the beginning-only dual binding of UR267A/B or the both-ends dual binding of UR273A/B. No other khipu in the analysed set carries a mid-length fragment.

evidence.test: 67.5 cm secondary-khipu attachment, OKR physical description

v3_contribution.claim: CLUSTER-TO-DEPTH HYPOTHESIS: 6 clusters with deep subtrees = six kurakas or six phases, each carrying subordinate team / sub-task detail across L2-L5. Consistent with the hierarchical-governance signature observed at the Incahuasi Kallanka.

OPPOSITE-WALL TWIN: UR280 at the north wall, UR263 at the south wall of the same Kallanka. Physical archive-layout pairing suggests administrative COMPLEMENT (one side tracking people, other side tracking buildings; or in-flows vs out-flows).

evidence.test: opposite-wall excavation co-location of UR280 and UR263

v3_contribution.claim: ASCHER VERIFICATION 2/3 (67 percent) is MODERATE and covering_ratio is LOW (0.08), consistent with a deep-tree ARCHIVE document where arithmetic is ornamental rather than primary. Contrast the audit-tier khipus (UR256 5/5, UR267B 8/10, UR275 5/5) whose arithmetic is central.

evidence.test: Ascher integrity profile vs audit-tier commodity khipus

companions.note: PHYSICAL TWIN across the hall: UR263 'Khipu Urton' at the opposite wall of the same Kallanka (Sector A, UE 10 equivalent locus). UR280 is the archaeological complement; a parallel deep-read of UR263 would confirm whether the two khipus divided the hall's administrative labour.

Incahuasi UE 17 cadastral-astronomical hybrid. Shares the deep-tree Incahuasi administrative convention (depth L3) but carries a richer vocabulary. UR280 is the minimal-vocab deep-tree; UR277 the rich-vocab mid-tree.

UE 18 peanut tier-3 district summary. UR268 has presentation binding (LK tassel + needlework bundle) and depth L4; UR280 has compound attachment binding and depth L5. Together they sketch the Incahuasi binding-convention spectrum.
UE 1 'Quipu del Mani' peanut tier-1 day-book. Shallow-and-wide (depth L3) in contrast to UR280's deep-and-narrow. Same site, opposite structural-signature pole of the archive.
UE 16 aji tier-1 day-book. Dual-bound at beginning only to UR267B. UR280's mid-length compound binding is the third binding-class after beginning-only (UR267A/B) and both-ends (UR273A/B).
UE 14 sparse event register (64/87 cords EMPTY). UR273A's both-ends binding and sparsity contrasts with UR280's compound binding and deep-tree density -- two Incahuasi compound-binding conventions.
Workshop work-register with kama + paki construction signature. UR280's kama + paki pairing is the same convention applied to the Kallanka hall itself rather than a workshop ledger.

word_reading.note: 4/4 dictionary-confirmed STRING readings (100 percent dict hit). chiqa, chaqa, kama, paki all stable and frequently attested in the corpus. The sparseness of the vocabulary is a document-design choice (deep-tree carries the information), not a reading weakness.

document_type.note: administrative_record classification supported by kama + paki governance-construction pair + Kallanka provenance + 6-cluster deep-tree signature. Sparse vocabulary limits certainty vs a verb-dense labor-tribute document. Sub-type unresolved: hall-ledger vs construction-register vs paired-amendment document.

narrative.note: The Kallanka-hall-ledger reading is internally coherent and anchored on Chu excavation record + Hyslop Kallanka architecture + Gonzalez Holguin kama/paki glosses. The 'six kurakas / six phases' interpretation of the six clusters is a best-fit hypothesis, not a decisive reading. The compound binding interpretation (paired amendment) is testable on museum inspection.

data_integrity.note: 2/3 Ascher sums verified (67 percent). covering_ratio LOW (0.08). 16 L? cords remain unresolved. Knot-level data mostly complete via KFG merge; the arithmetic layer is sparse so cannot be used to cross-check the deep-tree structure.

sections.title: Opening Verification-Delivery-Governance Triad
sections.reading: Numerical opening cords (p1-p5) resolve via KFG merge into raw quantities. At p6 the scribe writes chiqa on alpaca-brown: VERIFIED. The register is declared good. At p11 chaqa (still AB): DELIVERY / BRIDGE marker. At p12 the color shifts to NB and kama appears: GOVERN. The opening triad establishes the document's frame -- a verified-delivered-governed ledger.

key_cords.note: OPENING VERIFICATION -- the register is declared good
DELIVERY / BRIDGE -- second term of the triad
GOVERN -- governance marker on NB structural channel

sections.title: Numerical Body + Compound Binding
sections.reading: Twenty cords of pure numbers between the opening triad and the construction marker at p32. The deep subsidiary tree (L2-L5) hangs primarily from this block. At 67.5 cm from the primary-cord origin (physical measurement, approximately mid-length) a SECOND FRAGMENTARY KHIPU is tied onto the primary cord -- the compound binding. Not a subsidiary hanging vertically but a horizontal attachment of a separate document. Amendment, supplementary register, or letter-of-transmittal-in-knot-form.

key_cords.note: compound binding at 67.5 cm -- second fragmentary khipu tied on to primary cord

sections.title: Construction Marker
sections.reading: At p32, on NB (same structural channel as kama at p12), the scribe writes paki: BREAK / CONSTRUCT. The delayed construction mark closes the minimal administrative formula twenty cords after the opening triad. kama + paki on NB = governance + construction on the structural channel; chiqa + chaqa on AB = verification + delivery on the primary channel.

key_cords.note: CONSTRUCTION MARKER -- closes the minimal admin formula

sections.title: Closing Body + Deep Subtrees
sections.reading: The remaining L1 cords close the primary register. The DEEP SUBORDINATE TREES (L2 at 108 cords, L3 at 87 cords, L4 at 17 cords, L5 at 1 cord) carry the bulk of the document's information -- the subsidiary detail of kurakas, sub-tasks, workers, days, units. Six clusters of ~42 cords each, each cluster a deep subtree.

key_cords.note: last of the 37 L1 cords; subordinate trees (108 L2 + 87 L3 + 17 L4 + 1 L5) carry the document's information

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Ascher Sums as Erasure Codes. ALBA Project preprint.
Urton, G. & Chu, A. (2015). Accounting in the King's Storehouse: the Inkawasi Khipu Archive. LAQ 26(4).
Urton, G. (2017). Inka History in Knots. University of Texas Press.
Chu, A. (2013+). Proyecto Arqueologico Incahuasi excavation reports, Sector A UE 10 (Kallanka).
Hyslop, J. (1990). Inka Settlement Planning. University of Texas Press -- Kallanka architectural type.
OKR cord-note on UR280 (Urton): 'Last khipu excavated; Kallanka nearest the palacio, opposite wall from Khipu Urton (UR263). 67.5 cm fragmentary khipu tied on.'
Gonzalez Holguin, D. (1608). Vocabulario -- kama = 'crear, gobernar'; paki = 'romper, construir'.
Compare UR263 (same-Kallanka opposite-wall twin, to be deep-read), UR267A/B and UR273A/B (dual-binding conventions), UR177 (kama+paki workshop signature).
